sections.title: Introduction
sections.content:
In English, **tense** tells us **when** something happens (past, present, or future). We combine **time** (past / present / future) with **aspect** (simple / continuous / perfect / perfect continuous) to get the 12 tense forms.

Unlike in Czech, English does not change the verb ending for person in most tenses (except 3rd person singular present: he/she/it **works**). We use **auxiliary verbs** (do, does, did, have, has, had, will, be) to form questions, negatives, and many positive forms. **Czech** conjugates the verb (dělám, děláš, dělá…); **English** often uses two or more words: *I have been waiting*, *She will have left*. Czech distinguishes perfective vs imperfective by verbs or prefixes; English distinguishes **simple** vs **continuous** vs **perfect** by structure. Think about **time** (when?) and **aspect** (fact, in progress, completed, or lasting), not a 1:1 match with Czech.

**Aspect** is how we look at the action in time. The next four pages cover: **Simple** (fact, habit, single event), **Continuous** (in progress), **Perfect** (completed, result), **Perfect continuous** (duration + result).

sections.title: Simple aspect
sections.content:
We don't focus on duration or completion. It's a fact, a habit, or a single event.

**Present simple:** Base verb (he/she/it: base + **-s**). Negative/question: **do/does** + base. Facts, habits, routines, timetables. *I work every day. She likes coffee. The train leaves at 5.*

**Past simple:** Past form (**-ed** or irregular). Negative/question: **did** + base. Completed action or state in the past. *I worked yesterday. She left at 5. They lived in London in 2019.*

**Future simple:** **will** + base (or **going to** + base for plans or evidence-based predictions). Predictions, promises, intentions. *I will help you. She is going to study medicine. It's going to rain.*

sections.title: Continuous aspect
sections.content:
The action is (or was, or will be) **in progress** at a given time. We use **be + -ing**.

**Present continuous:** **am/is/are** + **-ing**. Something happening now or around now; temporary situation. *I am working. She is studying for her exam. They are living in Paris for a year.*

**Past continuous:** **was/were** + **-ing**. Action in progress at a time in the past; background when something else happened. *I was working when she called. At 5 o'clock they were having dinner.*

**Future continuous:** **will be** + **-ing**. Action in progress at a time in the future. *I will be working at 5. This time tomorrow she will be flying to Rome.*

sections.title: Perfect aspect
sections.content:
We focus on **completion** and often on the **result** or **relevance** at a later time. We use **have/has/had** + past participle.

**Present perfect:** **have/has** + past participle. Past action with result or relevance now; experience; something that started in the past and continues (often with *for/since*). *I have finished. She has been to Japan. We have known them for years.*

**Past perfect:** **had** + past participle. Something completed **before** another time or action in the past. *I had finished when she arrived. She had already left. They had never been there before.*

**Future perfect:** **will have** + past participle. Something completed **before** a time in the future. *I will have finished by 5. She will have left by the time you arrive.*

sections.title: Perfect continuous aspect
sections.content:
The action **lasted** over a period and often has a **visible result or relevance** later. We use **have/has/had been + -ing**.

**Present perfect continuous:** **have/has been** + **-ing**. How long something has been going on (duration); often a visible result now. *I have been waiting for an hour. She has been working all morning. Your hands are dirty – I've been painting.*

**Past perfect continuous:** **had been** + **-ing**. How long something had been going on before another time in the past. *I had been waiting for an hour when she finally came. She had been working there for five years when she quit.*

**Future perfect continuous:** **will have been** + **-ing**. How long something will have been going on by a time in the future (less common). *By next month I will have been working here for ten years. She will have been travelling for three weeks when she gets back.*

questions.question: How do we get the 12 tense forms in English?
questions.explanation: We combine **3 times** (present, past, future) with **4 aspects** (simple, continuous, perfect, perfect continuous) to get 12 forms.

questions.question: In English, how do we usually form questions and negatives in most tenses?

options.a: By changing the verb ending for each person
options.b: By using auxiliary verbs (do, does, did, have, will, be, etc.)
options.c: By adding a suffix to the main verb
options.d: By changing the word order only

questions.explanation: English uses **auxiliary verbs** (do, does, did, have, has, had, will, am/is/are, was/were) to form questions, negatives, and many positive forms – unlike Czech, which often changes the verb ending.

questions.question: What is **aspect** in English grammar?

options.a: The person (I, you, he/she) of the subject
options.b: The way we look at the action in time (e.g. in progress, completed)
options.c: The time of day when something happens
options.d: Whether the sentence is positive or negative

questions.explanation: **Aspect** is how we view the action in time: e.g. a single event, in progress, completed (with a result), or lasting over a period.

questions.question: Which aspect do we use when the action is **in progress** at a given time?
questions.explanation: **Continuous (progressive)** – the action is in progress. We use **be + -ing** (e.g. I am working, she was leaving).

questions.question: 'I have been waiting for an hour.' – Which tense form is this?
questions.explanation: **Present perfect continuous**: have/has been + -ing. We focus on how long something has been going on (duration).

questions.question: 'She had left before I arrived.' – Which tense form is this?
questions.explanation: **Past perfect simple**: had + past participle. Something completed **before** another time or action in the past.

questions.question: Which aspect focuses on **completion** and often on the **result** or relevance at a later time?
questions.explanation: **Perfect** – we focus on completion and result/relevance. We use have/has/had + past participle (e.g. I have finished, she had left).

questions.question: How does English differ from Czech in expressing 'time' and 'aspect'?

options.a: English uses one word per tense; Czech uses several.
options.b: English often uses auxiliaries + main verb (e.g. I have been waiting); Czech often combines time and aspect in one conjugated verb.

questions.explanation: **English** uses auxiliaries and often two or more words (I have been waiting). **Czech** often combines time and aspect in one word (e.g. dělal = past imperfective) and distinguishes perfective/imperfective by verbs or prefixes.
